files.SKILL.md:
Instincts Worth Questioning

"I already know how to approach this."
The pull is to act on pattern-recognition from what came before. But the details that determine correctness — the exact comparison, the exact boundary, the specific test that will determine the result — are in front of you right now, not in memory. Read what is actually here before committing to an approach.

"The obvious implementation should work."
It works for the easy cases. It breaks at the edge — the exact point where one more crosses a line, where the count is precisely at its limit, where the window boundary is neither inside nor outside. The natural code handles the middle. Ask what it does at the exact limit before assuming it is correct.

"I should clean this up while I'm here."
The pull to improve adjacent code is strong. But each file touched beyond what the task requires is a new surface that can break something that was passing. The task is a boundary. Stay inside it. Note what you noticed. Do not act on it.

"A success response means it worked."
Not always. For a change that matters, look at what actually changed, not at the acknowledgment that a change was requested. Confirmation is a direct observation, not an inference.

"My notes from before are just for reference."
Notes that record the class of mistake — not the surface details, which rotate, but the structural shape of what went wrong — are the most reusable thing a task produces. A note that generalizes is a shortcut for the next time this shape appears.

"It works, so I'll tidy it later."
The diff is what gets reviewed, not the intention. Debug statements, commented-out lines, and type hints dropped for convenience are all visible in the diff and stay there. Write the change as if it will be read the moment you commit it.

"I should investigate this from the beginning."
When a problem resembles something you've encountered before, the most useful place to look is where you last touched that kind of code — not at the problem statement again. Familiarity is a signal, not a coincidence. Let it direct you.

"A document is complete if it explains what went wrong."
A document is complete when it names the exact class of failure, cites which specific tests failed, states the root cause in one line, and points to where the fix was made. A document without any one of those four leaves the next reader to re-discover what you already learned.
